lessons.title: Lesson 1: Color is Your Best Clue
content: The single most reliable visual indicator of matcha quality is color.

High-grade ceremonial matcha is a deep, vibrant green. Lower-grade culinary matcha shifts toward yellow-green or olive tones. This happens because premium matcha is shade-grown longer, producing more chlorophyll.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Texture Tells the Story
content: Beyond color, powder fineness separates grades.

Ceremonial matcha is stone-ground to particles around 5–10 microns - finer than baby powder. It feels silky between your fingers. Culinary matcha has larger, grittier particles that are visible to the naked eye.
A quick 'smear test' on paper can reveal the difference.
summary_bullets: Color: Deep emerald green = high grade, yellow-olive = low grade
Texture: Silky fine powder = ceremonial, visible grains = culinary
Smear test: Smooth streak (good) vs grainy line (low)

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Proper Storage
content: Matcha is extremely sensitive to light, heat, and air. Even the highest quality matcha will dull and turn yellow if stored incorrectly.
Always store your matcha in an airtight, opaque container in a cool place (the fridge is best!). Once opened, it should be consumed within 1-2 months for peak flavor and color.
lessons.tip: Avoid glass jars! Sunlight is the number one enemy of vibrant green matcha.

lessons.title: 4 Matcha Grades
lessons.subtitle: Notice how color shifts from deep emerald to dull olive - and how texture coarsens.

grades.label: Grade A
grades.name: Premium Ceremonial
grades.desc: Pure emerald green + ultra-fine silky particles

grades.label: Grade B
grades.name: Ceremonial
grades.desc: Vibrant green + fine powder, slight texture

lessons.title: Matcha in Drinks
content: You can also judge matcha quality after it is whisked into a drink.
A ceremonial-grade latte will have a vivid, saturated green hue - think bright jade. A culinary-grade latte tends toward a muted, brownish or yellowish green.
The color difference becomes especially obvious in iced drinks, where the matcha sits against a white milk background.
lessons.tip: Always compare under the same light source for accuracy.

lessons.title: Foam
content: Higher-quality matcha produces more foam because it contains more saponins (natural foaming compounds) and is ground much finer, trapping air more effectively when whisked.
The result is a dense, creamy microfoam that holds its shape. Culinary-grade matcha produces little to no foam and a flat surface.

lessons.title: Review: Visual Checklist
bullets: Color: Deep emerald green. Avoid dull yellow or olive tones.
Texture: Silky fine powder (5-10 microns). Avoid gritty or visible grains.
Foam: Dense, creamy microfoam. Avoid flat, bubble-less surfaces.
Storage: Airtight, opaque container. Protect from light and heat.

lessons.title: Time for practice!
content: You've learned how to visually grade matcha by color, texture, and foam. Now put your skills to the test!

quiz_questions.title: Identify This Matcha
quiz_questions.text: Look at this sample. Is it ceremonial or culinary grade? Select your answer and give one visual reason.
quiz_questions.alt: A pile of bright emerald-green matcha powder - is this ceremonial or culinary grade?

correct.title: You are correct! 🎉
correct.body: This is a typical ceremonial grade matcha, with a vibrant green color and fine particles. Culinary matcha tends to look more yellowish and dull.

incorrect.title: You are wrong! 😭
incorrect.body: This is actually ceremonial grade matcha, which is brighter green and finer in texture. The vivid green hue and silky powder are the key giveaways.

quiz_questions.title: Rank These Matcha Samples
quiz_questions.text: Rank these three matcha powder samples from highest to lowest grade.
quiz_questions.alt: Three matcha powder samples side by side for ranking

correct.title: You are correct! 🎉
correct.body: The middle sample is highest quality due to its deep emerald green and fine texture. The right sample is slightly less vibrant but still good. The left sample is lowest quality due to yellow tones and coarser texture.
correct.tip: The more vibrant and saturated the green, the more chlorophyll from shade-growing, which signals a higher grade.

incorrect.title: You are wrong! 😭
incorrect.body: Correct answer: Middle > Right > Left. The middle sample's deep emerald green and fine texture mark it as the highest grade. Look for deeper green and finer texture to identify higher grade matcha.
incorrect.tip: The more vibrant and saturated the green, the more chlorophyll from shade-growing, which signals a higher grade.

quiz_questions.title: Which Bowl is Higher Quality?
quiz_questions.text: Which bowl was made with higher-grade matcha? Base your answer on color and foam.
quiz_questions.alt_left: Bowl X - ceremonial grade matcha with foam
quiz_questions.alt_right: Bowl Y - culinary grade matcha, flat surface

correct.title: You are correct! 🎉
correct.body: Bowl X has a brighter, more vivid jade green color, while Bowl Y leans darker and more olive-brown. Bowl X also has a fine, creamy froth with tiny uniform bubbles - only possible when the powder is ground finely enough to whisk properly.
correct.tip: In whisked matcha, froth quality is a bonus visual indicator on top of color.

incorrect.title: You are wrong! 😭
incorrect.body: Bowl X is higher quality because of its vibrant green color and smooth foam texture. Bowl Y's flat, foam-free surface suggests coarser particles that couldn't hold air.
incorrect.tip: In whisked matcha, froth quality is a bonus visual indicator on top of color.
